items.q: What does modulation mean in music?
items.correct: Changing from one key to another within a piece.
items.why: Students conflate modulation with any musical change. Specifically, modulation is a KEY CHANGE, usually established by a perfect cadence in the new key - a structural device.

items.correct: Two or more independent melodic lines sounding together.
items.misconception: A single melody with chord accompaniment.
items.why: Students confuse polyphony with thick texture or homophony. Polyphony specifically means INDEPENDENT MELODIES - like a fugue. Melody + chords is HOMOPHONY, not polyphony.

items.q: Which chords form a plagal cadence?
items.why: Students confuse plagal (IV-I) with perfect (V-I) because both end on I. The 'amen' at the end of hymns is the classic plagal cadence - subdominant to tonic, with a softer ecclesiastical feel.
It's 1865. “1855” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Schubert wrote over how many Lieder?
items.why: It's 600. “100” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Schubert lived 1797 to 1828. What was his age at death (in years)?
items.why: Exact answer: 31. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
